coredata.dc:description:
TiN-reinforced Ni–B composite coatings were synthesized in the watts bath using pulse electrodeposition method at different duty cycles of 20%, 40%, 60%, and 80%. The surface morphology, phase structure, hardness, wear, and corrosion behavior of the coatings produced at different duty cycles were examined. The effect of the duty cycle on the microstructure of the composite coating was investigated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis. Depending on the duty cycle, a coating thickness of ∼ 69.
7
μ m
was obtained on the cathode surface. Crystallite size and lattice distortion of coatings were calculated with X-ray diffraction (XRD) data. The nano-indentation hardness results showed that the high nucleation rate at 40% duty cycle increased the coating hardness to 1170 HV. The wear test results indicated that the distribution of TiN particles in the coating content reduced the wear rate of the coating to 2.121 × 10
− 5
mm
3/Nm. Corrosion tests performed in 3.5 wt.% NaCl solutions were analyzed by Tafel curves and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Depending on the duty cycle/reinforcement particle distribution relationship,
i
corr
and
E
corr
values were measured as 2.27x10 −6 (A/cm 2) and -0.427 V, respectively.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
TiN-reinforced Ni–B composite coatings were synthesized in the watts bath using pulse electrodeposition method at different duty cycles of 20%, 40%, 60%, and 80%. The surface morphology, phase structure, hardness, wear, and corrosion behavior of the coatings produced at different duty cycles were examined. The effect of the duty cycle on the microstructure of the composite coating was investigated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis. Depending on the duty cycle, a coating thickness of ∼ 69.
7
μ m
was obtained on the cathode surface. Crystallite size and lattice distortion of coatings were calculated with X-ray diffraction (XRD) data. The nano-indentation hardness results showed that the high nucleation rate at 40% duty cycle increased the coating hardness to 1170 HV. The wear test results indicated that the distribution of TiN particles in the coating content reduced the wear rate of the coating to 2.121 × 10
− 5
mm
3/Nm. Corrosion tests performed in 3.5 wt.% NaCl solutions were analyzed by Tafel curves and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Depending on the duty cycle/reinforcement particle distribution relationship,
i
corr
and
E
corr
values were measured as 2.27x10 −6 (A/cm 2) and -0.427 V, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: In industrial applications, strength, wear, and corrosion resistance are factors that affect the performance and service life of the material. Hence, an efficient and economical protective coating is applied to the material (Liu et al., 2020a). The electrodeposition process is preferred owing to its advantages such as simple usage, low cost, high efficiency, and high-temperature performance compared to other coatings (Li et al., 2019c). It is stated that the pulse electrodeposition used in electrodeposition processes produces a more compact coating with more particle content included in the matrix than conventional direct current electrodeposition because of the controllability of the coating process (Wasekar et al., 2016). As well as, Ni (Zhang et al., 2020) and Cr (Molodkina et al., 2020) as pure metal coatings, Ni–B (Taha-Tijerina et al., 2019), Ni–P (Lelevic and Walsh, 2019), Ni–Cr (Firouzi-Nerbin et al., 2020), and Ni–W (Wasekar et al., 2020b) alloy coatings with high mechanical and tribological properties are widely used. However, since these coatings are insufficient in industrial applications under harsh conditions, metal matrix composite coatings reinforced with reinforcing particles are preferred (Hou et al., 2014a). Particle-reinforced composite coatings are strengthened by fine grain size, and dispersion hardening occurs in the second phase particles (Wasekar et al., 2020a). Generally, Ni matrix composite coatings produced by electrodeposition or electroless processes are widely used because they provide superior performance in different industrial applications (Dehgahi et al., 2017). Mechanical, tribological, and anti-corrosion properties continue to be investigated on the Ni–B alloy matrix, which is used as a hard matrix in the automotive, electronics, aerospace, and petrochemical industries (Li and Zhang, 2020). Recently, studies on the hardness, wear, and corrosion resistance properties of the composite coating by including reinforcement particles such as SiC (Li et al., 2021), Al2O3 (Li et al., 2018), TiC (Li et al., 2023), and yttria-stabilized zirconia (YSZ) (Li et al., 2019a) to the Ni alloy matrix have been published in the literature. These particles, which are used as reinforcing elements, were well dispersed in the metal matrix, improving the mechanical properties and stability of the structure (Cho et al., 2018). Besides, another ceramic particle widely used in the literature, titanium nitride (TiN), is considered an alternative to protect the substrate material due to its higher hardness ( ∼ 2140 HV) and melting point (3273 K) (Ranjan et al., 2020). Besides, it has been stated that the hardness of the coatings depends on the hardness of the ceramic particles embedded in the matrix (Ma et al., 2020). Cui et al. (2018) deposited high microhardness TiN particles on Ni by electrodeposition. Umapathi et al. (2020) declared that the mechanical and tribological properties of Ni–P–TiN coatings deposited on steel increased. Yan et al. (2020) reported that TiN particles embedded in the coating increased the coating hardness and wear resistance with grain refining effect. Consequently, in this study, as an alternative study to surface treatment applications, TiN particles have been embedded in the Ni–B alloy matrix in different duty cycles by pulsed electrodeposition method, and the morphology, structure, mechanical, tribological, and corrosive medium properties of the composite coating have been investigated.

TiN ceramic particles were added to the Ni–B matrix by the pulsed electrodeposition method, and the electrolyte composition and the operation parameters are given in Table 1. 2 mm thick, 40 mm × 30 mm mild steel as cathode samples and 4 mm thick, 60 mm × 40 mm nickel plate as anode samples were prepared. Steel substrates were ground with 240, 400, 600, 800, and 1200 sand emery papers, respectively, before electrodeposition. The ground substrates were activated by washing in HCl solution for 30 seconds at room temperature, then washed with distilled water. TiN powder with particle size <
1
μ m
was used as reinforcement material. It was subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 30 minutes to suspend TiN particles in solution before electrodeposition, then added to the coating solution and mixed mechanically for about 1 h. Ultrasonic mixing allowed the TiN particles to break down and the particle size to be reduced. Before the electrodeposition, the cathode was chosen from a mild steel and an anode as a Ni plate with a distance of 3 cm between electrodes, and the pH of the electrolyte solution was fixed at 4.0. After electrodeposition, samples were heat-treated at Argon gas medium at 400 °C for 1 h. The plating system of the electrodeposition process are given in Fig. 1a. The pulse current mode and parameters applied during the electrodeposition process are shown in Fig. 1b. r,
i
p
,
i
a
T
o n ,
and
T
o f f
represent the duty cycle, peak current density, average current density, pulse on and pulse off, respectively.

Surface morphology and cross-sectional images of composite coatings were analyzed using SEM (JEOL 6060LV). Average surface roughness (
R
a
) of composite coatings was measured using a surface profilometer device (KLA Tencor-P6) from five different regions at a scanning speed of 1 mm/s. The crystalline orientation and the average crystallite size of the composite coatings were determined using XRD (Rigaku D/MAX 2000) with Cu K α radiation (1.54059 Å) in 2 θ (20°–80°) at 1°/min. The crystallite size was calculated according to Scherrer’s equation given in Eq. (1).
(1)
d =
k λ
β cos θ
where d represents the crystalline size, k is a constant coefficient (0.94), λ is X-ray wavelength (0.15418 nm), β is FWHM in radian, θ is Bragg angle of the peak. Hardness was measured with Anton Paar Nanoindentation Tester: NHT3 device with Berkovich indenter for 10 seconds under 50 mN applied load. The measurements were taken from five different points for each sample, and the mean value was calculated. Friction and wear tests were carried out at room temperature under dry sliding conditions using a Tribometer (CSM Instruments TRB 18-317). The wear tests of composite coatings were carried out in dry sliding conditions throughout 750 m sliding distance under a load of 5 N with a sliding speed of 20 cm/s at room temperature (25 °C). Al2O3 ball with a diameter of 6 mm was chosen as the counter-face. Low- and high-magnification images of worn surfaces were analyzed using SEM (JEOL 6060LV). Also, the 3D surface profiles of the wear tracks were examined using the KLA Tencor-P6 surface profilometer measuring device. Corrosion resistances, potentiodynamic polarization curves, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy tests (EIS) were analyzed using the Gamry Reference 3000 potentiostat model device. In electrochemical tests, in a three-electrode cell, corrosion calculations were performed in 3.5 wt. % NaCl solution by weight using a composite coating sample for the working electrode, graphite as the counter electrode, saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. For polarization curves obtained from EIS measurements, potential scanning frequencies in the range of −1.0 V and + 1.0 V at 1 mVs−1 scanning speed at room temperature were performed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of duty cycle on morphology
ce:section.ce:para:
SEM images of Ni–B–TiN composite coatings prepared at r = 20%, 40%, 60%, and 80% are shown in Fig. 2. As seen in the figure, when the duty cycle was increased from 20% to 40%, TiN particles on the coating surface were dispersed more densely and uniformly. TiN particles embedded in the coating prevented the growth of the crystal, creating new nucleation sites. Thus, the protrusions on the coating surface were reduced, and proportional growth occurred over the coating surface. Moreover, during the
T
o f f
period, the microstructure of the coating was controlled and adsorption and desorption occurred by recrystallization (Zhang and Lin, 2019). It could also be concluded that during the
T
o f f
, more particles were transferred from high-density areas to low-density areas on the cathode surface, more particles were deposited on the cathode surface, and a compact structure was formed so that TiN particles were evenly distributed in the metallic matrix. When the duty cycle was further increased, the particle content in the alloy matrix decreased, as poor TiN particles could reach double layers in a shorter
T
o f f
. However, as the particles agglomerated during the short
T
o f f
period, they caused larger particles to form on the coating surface. The agglomeration of TiN particles occurred around the projection on the surface below the higher electric field, leading to deposits of coarse and porous structures (Yang and Cheng, 2013). Moreover, it was stated in the literature that the protrusions observed in the 60% and 80% duty cycles increase the surface roughness of vertical growth (Li and Zhang, 2020). Ma et al. stated (Ma et al., 2020) that the duty cycle in the constant pulse frequency changes in direct proportion to the pulse width and that the metal ions in the electrolyte move continually towards the cathode surface in the high duty cycle. However, they reported that the particle content embedded in the coating was reduced since the metal ions were less likely to seize reinforcing particles. An instant current density greater than the current density was used without consuming metal ions on the electrode surface. This supported the initiation of the nucleation process and increased the number of grains per unit area to form finer-grained structures.

The average surface roughness (
R
a
) of composite coatings deposited at different duty cycles has been shown in Fig. 3a. As seen in the figure, the surface roughness of the coating deposited in the 40% duty cycle appeared to be lower compared to other coatings. This could be related to the low porous structure on the coating surface and the TiN particles filling the gaps of the Ni–B matrix on the cathode surface. The reduction in surface roughness observed by further increasing the duty cycle could be explained by the non-uniform distribution of ions on the substrate surface and the agglomeration of the reinforcing particles on the cathode surface, concerning the reduction in
T
o f f
(Gadhari and Sahoo, 2014). Shakoor et al. (2017) reported that the reinforcement particles added to the alloy matrix affect the surface roughness of the coating by the indirect grain refinement effect. The average TiN particle contents embedded in the Ni–B alloy matrix determined from EDS results for composite coatings are given in Fig. 3b. When the duty cycle during coating was increased to 40%, it was seen that the composite coating has a particle content of ∼ 13 wt. %. Zheng et al. (2017) reported that with an increase in
T
o n
, the particle content decreased. Accordingly, it could be said that if the
T
o n
exceeded 40%, it caused a decrease in the content of TiN particles deposited on the substrate.

Cross-sectional SEM images of composite coatings deposited at different duty cycles are shown in Fig. 4. The absence of defects such as gaps and cracks in the interface of all coatings between the substrate and the coating indicated that adhesion was sufficient. It was seen that the particle content embedded in the coating increased with increasing the duty cycle up to 40%, and the particles started to agglomerate with the further increasing duty cycle. The homogeneous dispersion and density of TiN particles embedded in the matrix in the composite coating at a 40% duty cycle could be attributed to high surface energy particles that supported more nucleation sites and inhibited the growth of crystals (Zhang et al., 2019). Wasekar et al. (2016) stated that the thickness of the coating increased with the increase in the duty cycle. They also reported that the particle density on the cathode surface increased with increasing
T
o f f
, thereby including more particles in the coating. The agglomeration of the particles increased as the current applied to the unit area of the cathode surface was greater (80% duty cycle). Besides, as the deposition time on the cathode surface increased with increasing duty cycle, the thickness of the composite coating increased to about ∼ 69.
7
μ m
. On the contrary, TiN particles reached the cathode surface less, and the amount of particles embedded in the matrix decreased (20% duty cycle).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of duty cycle on the phase constituent and crystallite size
ce:section.ce:para:
The effects of duty cycle on the phases and constituents of the Ni–B–TiN composite coatings are shown in the XRD diffraction patterns given in Fig. 5a. After the composite coating production at different duty cycles, samples have been subjected to heat treatment, and Ni2B, and Ni3B intermetallics were observed in the Ni matrix with co-deposited TiN ceramic particles. Diffractions of Ni crystals were observed at 44.5°, 51.8°, and 76.3°, which correspond to the (111), (200), and (220) planes, respectively. Nickel borides (Ni2B, Ni3B) formed by the dissolution of the B element in the Ni matrix and the embedding of TiN particles in the alloy matrix affected the peak intensity and width of the peak (Li et al., 2020). In all the coatings, Ni (111) preferential orientation showed the highest peak intensity, while the peak intensities of the (200) and (220) planes were lower. When the duty cycle increased from 20% to 40%, the intensity of Ni plane diffraction peaks showed an increase up to a certain duty cycle level, then exhibited a decreasing tendency. TiN particle content accommodated at the crystalline grain boundaries and the density of Ni2B and Ni3B phases increased (40% duty cycle), with the increase in the nucleation rate of the Ni crystals. The peak width changes of composite coatings deposited at different duty cycles in the highest density (111) plane were shown in Fig. 5b. According to the diffraction profile expressed in Gaussian form, it was seen that the peak broadening was highest in the coating deposited (40% duty cycle). In addition, when the crystallite size decreased, the internal pressure exerted by the surface tension created a stress field that affected the lattice tension (Safi and Bose, 2015). The crystal sizes of the composite coatings calculated by the Scherrer (Eq. (1)) equation based on the peak (111) at 44.5° are shown in Fig. 5c. The crystallite size of the coating deposited with a duty cycle of 40% was calculated to be below 50 nm. The decrease in crystallite size could be explained by increase in ceramic particle density at the grain boundaries, the increased TiN particle content included in the alloy matrix, and the inhibition of Ni crystal growth (Masoudi et al., 2014; Liang et al., 2011). Lattice distortions of the composite coating prepared at different duty cycles are shown in Fig. 5d. Lattice distortion results were calculated using Eqs. (2) and (3). The lattice constant could be calculated for an FCC system as follows:
(2)
a
2
=

2
4 s i

The deflection in the lattice constant was calculated using the following equation:
(3)
Lattice
Distortion
( % )
= 100

a
1

where
a
1
is the lattice constant for composite coatings produced with different current densities and,
a
0
is the lattice constant of pure Ni depositing. In the literature, lattice distortion of particle reinforced MMCs has been explained by lattice parameter mismatch, thermal stresses, and residual stress (El-Sherik et al., 2005). Li et al. (2019b) stated that the reinforcement particles included in the matrix caused lattice distortion. Jiang et al. (2018) reported that the second phase particles in the coating created residual stress, which caused lattice deterioration. It could be concluded that lattice distortion increased in preferential orientation as embedding TiN particles in the Ni–B alloy matrix caused lattice expansion in the (111) plane. In addition, Chen et al. (2020) stated that the size of B atoms was smaller than that of Ni-FCC; therefore, B atoms dissolved in the crystal lattice and caused lattice distortion. Low lattice distortion in the (200) and (220) planes could be explained by the fact that the reinforcement particles were smaller in the crystal structure. Also, the lattice degradation of the composite material was explained by the lattice mismatch between the substrate interface and the coating. The other reason was residual or actual stresses from certain coating conditions (Ranganatha et al., 2010).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of duty cycle on hardness
ce:section.ce:para:
Hardness, elastic modulus, and load–depth curves of Ni–B–TiN coatings produced by pulsed electrodeposition at different duty cycles are shown in Fig. 6, respectively. At 20% duty cycle (long
T
o f f
), the attraction force between the cathode surface and the particles surrounded by the ion cloud caused TiN particles to be separated from the substrate surface without being embedded in the matrix, thereby producing low coating hardness (Fig. 6a). Li et al. (2019b) attributed the hardness change in the composite coating to the particle content in the metal matrix. In addition, Sadreddini et al. (2015), Rahman et al. (2016) stated that the particles dispersed uniformly in the matrix caused grain refinement, and the hardness increased with the decrease in grain size. The hardness (1170 HV) of the Ni–B/TiN composite coating could be indicated by the higher rate of nucleation than the crystalline growth rate with the increase in duty cycle compared to other coatings (40% duty cycle). This increase in coating hardness could be explained by alloy matrix solid solution hardening, matrix grain refinement, and reinforcing phase induced hardening (Zhang et al., 2015). Dispersion hardening caused by highly homogeneously dispersed TiN particles in the matrix increased the hardness of the composite coating. Furthermore, it increased the hardness of the coating by preventing the movement of the dislocations with increased content of particles in the coating (Liang et al., 2011). Pancrecious et al. (2018) stated that the boron dissolved in the nickel matrix affected the stiffness increase. It could be stated that the increase in of co-deposited TiN particles was caused by an increase in the content of Ni2B and Ni3B phases that were detectable in the XRD analysis given in Fig. 5a. As the current density was applied to the unit area of the cathode, there was not enough time for the new grains to grow. Thus, there was also a reduction in the content of TiN particles embedded in the matrix of the composite coating. The mechanical properties of TiN-reinforced Ni–B composite coatings were analyzed by the load-penetration depth curves given in Fig. 6b. The increase in the penetration depth according to the indentation method showed that the hardness value of the material decreased (Oliver and Pharr, 1992). Chitsaz-Khoyi et al. (2020) reported that the reinforcing particles included in the coating increased the hardness and elastic modulus of the material by filling the porosities and voids in the coating. It could also be noted that the hardness of the composite coating decreased as the agglomerated TiN particles did not sufficiently resist the applied load. Moreover, the nanoindentation technique has been used in the elastic modulus measurements of Ni–B–TiN​ composite coatings, and the results are shown in Fig. 6c. The highest value of elastic modulus (310 GPa) was measured in the coating deposited at 40% duty cycle. As shown in the cross-sectional microstructure given in Fig. 4b, because of the homogeneous distribution and the highest particle content, the coating yielded the highest hardness and strength compared with the other coatings studied. The increase in the elastic modulus could be explained by the absorption of the stress applied to the material by the reinforcing particles as a result of the uniform dispersion of TiN particles embedded in the Ni–B alloy matrix (Khazeni et al., 2019). The elastic modulus was reduced to 250 GPa by increasing the duty cycle to 80%. It has been observed that
T
o n
was greater than optimum, it caused a reduction of TiN particles on the cathode surface and alloy matrix, thus increasing the penetration depth of the low-strength coating.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tribology of composite coatings deposited at different duty cycles
ce:section.ce:para:
The worn surface images at low magnification obtained from SEM micrographs of the composite coatings deposited at different duty cycles are shown in Fig. 7. Composite coating produced with 40% duty cycle has relatively less wear trace width (
428
μ m
) compared to other coatings. This could be explained by the fact that high TiN particle content resulted in decreased direct metal-counterface ball contact, and the wear resistance of the coating increased because of the limiting contact pressure during friction (Shao et al., 2012). In addition, increased TiN particle content deposited on the cathode surface during
T
o f f
prevented surface peeling and plastic deformation. Dispersion of reinforcing particles on the coating surface on the protrusions and therefore inability to reduce shear stress increased the damage on the wear trace (80% duty cycle) (Yin et al., 2016). As seen in Figs. 7g and 7j, peeling caused by adhesive wear occurred in the areas marked with yellow. Low hardness of the coating caused peeling on the sliding wear surface by the high plastic deformation mechanism and adhesive wear (Xu et al., 2020).

Hatipoglu et al. (2016) reported that the cracks on the wear surface were caused to generate delamination layers that were formed by the adhesion of wear residues. Cracks on the coating surface could be attributed to low coating hardness and residues on the surface (Fig. 7b). Xu et al. stated (Xu et al., 2020) that the hard particles in the composite coating suppressed the dislocation movement, and preventing shape deformation reduced the damage caused by the counter-surface. There was a decrease in surface cracks and plastic deformation with an increase in the hardness of the composite coating (Fig. 7e). The increased wear resistance of the coating could be attributed to the decreased inter-particle distance (40%); therefore, greater amount of load was transferred to the stiffer TiN particles, and surface damage, especially plastic deformation, was decreased. In addition, Pohler et al. (2015) stated that high coating hardness increased the wear of the counter-part alumina for coatings with similar chemical compositions, and that changes in surface morphology and phase composition affected wear behavior depending on the duty cycle. It was attributed to the nickel borides that formed after the heat treatment applied to the coating was less affected. Structural defects such as porosities and gaps formed at the deposited layer could not be filled by TiN particles, and it could be said that crack growth and delamination-type wear occur, as seen in Fig. 7h. The adhesive deformation mechanism and the plastic deformation of the surface caused by wear residues have been shown in Fig. 7k. Increasing the
T
o n
decreased the reinforcement particle content in the coating layer, and the reduced coating hardness led to an increased amount of plastic deformation. An increase in plastic deformation and, therefore, deformation hardening, caused an increase in the size of the cracks and the formation of more delamination-type wear. In 3D profilometer surface analysis, the reduction of the depth and width of the wear paths by increasing the duty cycle to 40% could be explained by the formation of a protective layer by the TiN particles, reducing defects in the structure and preventing direct contact with the coated surface. On the contrary, TiN particle content adsorbed to the cathode surface decreased, and the defective structures that formed on the surface resulted in an increase in the depth of the wear trace (Fig. 7l). The wear rates of the composite coatings at different duty cycles have been shown in Fig. 8a. According to Archard’s law, the wear rate is proportional to the hardness of the material (Wang et al., 2005). Also, hard solid particles with high hardness increase the wear resistance of the coating (Hou et al., 2014b). As stated in Fig. 3b, the increase in the content of particles included in the composite coating reduced the wear rate of the coating (2.121 × 10
− 5
mm
3/Nm). In addition, TiN particles increased the fatigue life of the coating by preventing the formation of defects such as cracks. As the particle content included in the coating decreased, the hardness of the coating decreased, and the poor layer that limited the contact area between the coating surface and the counter-face increased the wear rate to 4.356 × 10
− 5
mm
3/Nm. The friction coefficient values of the Ni–B–TiN composite coating have been shown in Fig. 8b. Torabinejad et al. (2017) stated that after the increase in initial wear, the surface becomes smooth and stabilized with an increased contact area. They also described the fluctuations in the friction coefficient as the localized fracture of the wear surface layer and the aggregation of wear residues. While the wear continued, the friction coefficient increased steadily. Increasing fluctuations with the increase in the duty cycle could be associated with an increase in plastic deformation and delamination layers. The high coefficient of friction could be attributed to deformed residues on the wear surface. The reduction in friction coefficient could be explained by the effect of the dense layer on the coating surface and the hardness of the TiN particles included in the coating. Also, the reduction of the shear stress of the TiN reinforcement particles reduced the coefficient of friction of the composite coating (Peng et al., 2017). The decrease in
T
o f f
could be indicated by the small amount of reinforcement particles adsorbed on the coating surface having a weak effect on the coating hardness and the increase in the coefficient of friction. The wear mechanism of composite coatings has been illustrated in Fig. 8c. The low hardness of the composite produced at 20% duty cycle caused plastic deformation. Considering that TiN ceramic particles were homogeneously distributed (40%) in the Ni-matrix, TiN particles on the composite surface were extruded and spreaded during the sliding process. Duty cycle affected that ceramic particle areas on the composite surface, which decreased, and because of agglomeration of particles and fatigue cracks, surface deformations occurred.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of duty cycle on the corrosion resistance
ce:section.ce:para:
Potentiodynamic polarization curves of composite coatings after immersion in 3.5 wt.% NaCl solution for 1 h have been shown in Fig. 9a. Corrosion current (
i
c o r r
) and corrosion potential (
E
c o r r
) values have been calculated from the Tafel curve that is listed in Table 2. Positive
E
c o r r
demonstrates low corrosion tendency, and
i
c o r r
shows low corrosion rate (Liu et al., 2020b).
E
c o r r
values of the composite coatings (20%, 40%, 60%, and 80%) were determined as -561 V, −427 V, −509 V, and −557 V, respectively. Taking into account
i
c o r r
values, it was seen that the coating deposited with 40% duty cycle has the lowest corrosion rate compared to other coatings. Kamnerdkhag et al. (2017) reported that as the duty cycle decreased, the corrosion potential of the coating shifted positively and the corrosion current density decreased. Also, Goldasteh and Rastegari (2014) reported that the composite coating increased the corrosion resistance of the matrix by preventing the local corrosion of the matrix because of uniform dispersion of the particles included in the structure. With Increasing the duty cycle to 40%, TiN particles distributed uniformly in the Ni–B alloy matrix prevented corrosive ion transfer to the cathode surface. Therefore, the active areas of the corrosive ions on the coating surface were reduced, and the corrosion prevention ability of the composite coating increased (Radwan et al., 2015). Compact structure and thick coating were effective in prevent the transition of Cl
−
ions to the substrate surface (Duan et al., 2007). Duty cycle affected that the growth rate of the grains on the cathode surface became higher than the formation rate of new nucleation areas. Metal ions that moved rapidly to the cathode surface formed protrusive structures, and the transport of reinforcing particles was less. In addition, the corrosion damage caused by the Cl
−
ions in the corrosive medium through the pores in certain areas on the substrate caused lower corrosion resistance in larger porous coatings.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) has been used to compare the corrosion capabilities of coatings produced at different duty cycles. Nyquist curves of the composite coatings produced at different duty cycles have been shown in Fig. 9b. Corrosion resistance develops as the impedance semicircle diameter increases, and the diameter of the Nyquist curve shows the polarization resistance of the electrode exposed to corrosion (He et al., 2018). The radius of the coating produced with 40% duty cycle was wider than that of other coatings. The corrosion resistance of the material was improved by ensuring that the morphology on the cathode surface of the TiN reinforcing particles added to the Ni–B alloy matrix was uniform and dense. Also, increase in the radius increased the response of the barrier layer to the corrosive environment (Guo et al., 2008). Zhang et al. (2019) stated that the coating increased the corrosion prevention capability of TiN particles, which were included in the coating, by supporting nucleation sites and preventing existing crystal growth. Weak transport of TiN particles to the coating surface and non-uniform grain growth created defective structures on the coating surface, and the corrosion ability of the coating decreased.

The equivalent electrical circuits (EEC) model has been shown in Fig. 9c, where
R
s
,
R
ct
and CPE
dl
show solution resistance, charge transfer resistance, and double-layer capacitance, respectively. The EEC model has been used to fit Nyquist curves. Corrosion parameters of composites produced at different duty cycles have been listed in Table 3. The resistance of the barrier layer to the corrosive environment could be neglected since the ions in the corrosive medium do not penetrate the substrate/interface when calculating the electrical equivalent over constant time (Fadaee and Javidi, 2014). It have been shown that the Rs and Rct values in Table 3 increased significantly when the duty cycle to 40%. Hakimizad et al. (2017) reported that the particles included in the coating improved the surface morphology by reducing the porosity in the coating and improved corrosion resistance of the coating. TiN particles included in the coating form a compact and uniform structure, filling the pores and cracks in the structure, and thereby improving the anti-corrosion capability of the coating by preventing corrosive ions from penetrating through the electrolyte solution (Bordbar-Khiabani et al., 2019). Despite the increase in the duty cycle, the decrease in
R
s
and
R
ct
values showed that Cl
−
ions diffuse through the pores. Sreekanth et al. (2013) reported that excessive porous structure on the coating surface means higher capacitance value. The highest
R
c
and
R
ct
values and the lowest CPE
dl
values in the composite coating were related to the microstructural compatibility of the structure. CPE
dl
value of the coating deposited at 40% duty cycle was lower than that of other coatings. Therefore, it could be concluded that the composite coating has the highest anti-corrosion capability. Despite the increase in duty cycle and the increase in coating thickness, the decrease in CPE
dl
value was attributed a sign of high porous structure.

Depending on the morphology of the structure, the corrosion behaviors of the composite coatings have been shown schematically in Fig. 10. Corrosion resistance of metal matrix composites (MMC) has been explained by microstructural changes and thickness (Zhu and Hihara, 2010; Bai et al., 2019). The matrix exhibited anodic behavior when the composite coating was exposed to a corrosive solution. The nickel boride phases and TiN particles in the Ni matrix filled the voids in dense structure and prevented the co-ionic ions in the electrolyte solution from diffusing towards the substrate surface. However, porous structures had open areas where Cl-ions​ penetrated, and the corrosion resistance of the coating decreased (Kong et al., 2018). Corrosion attack was more conductive, and corrosion resistance decreased in relation to these pores, and absence of cracks in the structure formed by closing the pores with the reinforcing particles increased the corrosion resistance. The ions in the corrosive medium easily diffused into the substrate/coating interface, reducing the anti-corrosion properties of the coating. The thick and non-porous coating made it difficult to transfer corrosive ions and prevented the substrate from being eroded.

Furthermore, the microstructures of the composite coating deposited with 40% duty cycle before and after the corrosion test have been shown in Fig. 11. The images taken from the corroded surfaces of the coatings showed that the cauliflower structure has not changed greatly and that gap in the coating surface was almost nonexistent. The absence of porous structure on the corroded surface can be associated with the filling of the voids by TiN particles on the coating surface.

Depositions of Ni–B composite coatings reinforced with TiN reinforcement particles at different duty cycles were successfully accomplished by the pulsed electrodeposition method. The effects of duty cycle change in the composite coatings on morphology, phase structure, hardness, wear, and corrosion were investigated. The surface morphology of the composite coating produced with a 40% duty cycle contained denser, smoother, and evenly dispersed fine-grained TiN particles. Composite coatings that were heat-treated at 400 °C for 1 h showed precipitation of higher contents of Ni2B and Ni3B crystal phases, which are extremely beneficial to prevent wear and corrosion. With grain refinement and dispersion hardening, TiN particles accommodated at the grain boundaries of the crystal increased the hardness of the composite coating. In the wear tests carried out under constant load, TiN particles have reduced the contact area of the coating surface with the abrasive surface and improved the wear resistance of the coating. The lowest wear rate in Ni–B–TiN composite coatings was calculated at 2.121 × 10
− 5
mm
3/Nm in the coating deposited at 40% duty cycle. The period of the
T
o f f
in the duty cycle affected the metal ions and reinforcement particles in the electrolyte that adsorb to the cathode surface. In addition, it was understood that increasing coating thickness with an increasing duty cycle was not effective alone in preventing corrosion. Therefore, it was stated that the coating thickness and non-porous structure were important to improve the anti-corrosion capability of the composite coating. In this way, TiN particles in the composite coating with its dense structure and homogeneous particle distribution prevented the diffusion of corrosive ions into the coating by creating a protective barrier on the substrate surface. As a result, we have proposed that the composite coating with superior mechanical, tribological, and anti-corrosion properties obtained by providing a compact, smooth, and homogeneous distribution of TiN particles in the Ni–B alloy matrix can be used in different industrial areas.
